texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CO2 stripping from water is considered an upcoming technology in the  field  of  environmental  engineering.  It  has  mainly  been  used  for carbon  capture  and  storage  (CCS)  systems  because  CO2 should  be stripped  from  the  absorbent  for  carbon  storage  and  further  carbon capture [1]. The absorbents in each operation type (e.g., physical and chemical absorption) need to strip CO2  from the solvent for absorbent regeneration [2 -4]. CO2  stripping from water plays an important role not only in the CCS process, but recently, this technology has also been utilized in industrial processes to reduce potential performance degradation by removing dissolved CO2. It is often used to prevent dissolved CO2 from  degrading  the  electrodeionistation  (EDI)  process  in  the manufacture  of  ultra-pure  water  (UPW)  [5].  The  technology  is  also adopted to reduce scaling formation in a water treatment system [6 -10].

texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Carbon capture and storage (CCS) and industrial processes are driving the demand for CO2 stripping from water. Membrane contactors are considered a favorable solution to the conventional challenges of CO2 stripping from water,  such  as  inefficiency  and  high  space  occupancy.  However,  the  factors  affecting  membrane  contactor performance  have  not  been  systemically  investigated  in  the  context  of  efficient  CO2 stripping.  This  study analyzed the factors affecting the membrane contactors for the CO2 stripping process based on experiments and theoretical modeling. The factors are classified according to advection (e.g., liquid flow rate), diffusion (e.g., vacuum pressure and pH), and membrane (e.g., long-term operation and arrangement). A high liquid flow rate increased the CO2  stripping flux, whereas it decreased the CO2  removal efficiency. At vacuum pressure, CO2 stripping  depended on the vacuum pressure but it was not effective in the air-combination mode. It is also important to maintain pH 5 as pH significantly affects the diffusion. In the long-term experiment, membrane fouling  and pore swelling were not observed to cause performance degradation. Further, parallel and series designs for membrane contactors were compared, the parallel design exhibits superior CO2  stripping performance. This study provides strategies for using membrane contactors for efficient CO2  stripping.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane contactors are a promising method for CO2 stripping. It can solve the traditional challenge of column-type stripper such as low efficiency,  high  energy  consumption,  and  high  space  occupancy  requirements [14,15]. The membrane process can increase the effective contact area between the gas and liquid phases, which leads to a high stripping  performance.  In  addition,  the  membrane  contactor  can  be operated at temperatures between 80 ◦ C and 100 ◦ C, which is significantly  lower  than  that  of  the  column  stripper.  The  application  of  a membrane contactor in CO2  stripping can be beneficial for both stripping performance and energy consumption; a variety of studies have investigated this application [2,14,16].

texts.orig: Mansourizadeh et al. [17 -19] studied CO2 stripping from water using a porous polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) membrane. They modified the membrane surface with polyethylene glycol 400 (PEG-400) and ortho --phosphoric acid (PA) as additives. Rahbari-Sisakht et al. [20,21] applied a porous asymmetric polysulfone (PSf) membrane and a surface-modified membrane (SMM) to enhance the CO2 stripping flux. DashtArzhandi et al. increased the hydrophobicity of membranes with montmorillonite  (MMT)  additives  [22].  Thus,  CO2 stripping  performance  can  be  improved  by  increasing  the  hydrophobicity.  Further, Bazhenov et al. [23] investigated the CO2  stripping performance from various ionic liquids (ILs) which can play the role of CO2  absorbent, recently.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  factors  affecting  membrane  contactors  were  not  previously analyzed systemically in experimental or theoretical works. The effects of the liquid flow rate on the wetting phenomenon and CO2 removal rate were not previously identified. Because the liquid flow rate is a major condition affecting the performance of a membrane contactor, it should be analyzed experimentally, with these results being adopted for theoretical modeling. In addition, previous studies utilized N2 sweep gas for experiments, but this is not economically feasible for commercial industrial operations. The performance of membrane contactors should be analyzed  for  practical  application  in  both  the  air-combination  and vacuum mode. In contrast, the pH greatly affects the performance of the membrane contactor because dissolved CO2 transforms into H2CO3*(aq), HCO3 (aq), and CO3 2 (aq)  as a function of the pH of the solution [9,10,24]. Thus, it is critical to analyze the change in the pH for CO2  stripping. Membrane performance can decrease over time owing to fouling and pore swelling [25]. Long-term operational efficiency of the membrane contactor is necessary for successful CO2  stripping from water. Meanwhile,  the  CO2 removal  efficiency  associated  with  the  membrane arrangement has not been studied. Therefore, theoretical modeling is required  to  present  high-performance  membrane  configurations  for practical applications.

texts.orig: In this study, the factors affecting membrane contactors to improve CO2 stripping from water were investigated through experimental and theoretical analyses. The membrane wetting constant and CO2 removal efficiency were identified at different liquid flow rates to improve CO2 stripping by advection. Second, factors affecting diffusion, such as vacuum pressure and the pH condition, were identified. The effect of vacuum pressure was analyzed in air-combination and vacuum modes, and the pH conditions were controlled to achieve a high CO2 stripping flux. The  polypropylene  (PP)  membrane,  which has a membrane contact angle of approximately 100 ◦ , is commonly used for anti-wetting [26 -28]. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3. CO2 stripping experiment
texts.text: 2.3. CO2 stripping experiment

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The CO2 stripping experiment was conducted to investigate the CO2 stripping performance, as shown in Fig. 1. The rich CO2 solution flowed into the hollow fiber membrane contactor through the peristaltic pump. The  pH  was  adjusted  using  H2SO4  dosing.  The  liquid  flow  rate  and vacuum pressure were the standard conditions in this process to mitigate damage to the membrane. The flow rate at the liquid phase was set below 20 mL/min considering its breakthrough pressure ( Δ Pbreak ), while the vacuum pressure was above 0.3 atm. The air flowed into the gas inlet of the membrane contactor via the vacuum pump, and CO2  was then carried to the gas outlet. The desorbed CO2 from the water through the membrane contactor was measured using a CO2 gas analyzer installed at the  end  of  the  gas  phase.  To  obtain  the  stable  CO2 stripping  flux, experimental data were used after 15 min. While performance can be improved significantly by solvent heating, which drives the evaporation of  liquid  and  leads  to  the  wetting  phenomenon  [32],  this  step  was excluded  to  prevent  membrane  deformation  and  to  achieve  a  more energy-efficient process [26].

Despite  considering  the  breakthrough pressure in long-term operations, it is difficult to prevent the wetting phenomenon completely [38]. Most membranes were confirmed to be partially-wetted during operation, as shown in Fig. 3b. The partially-wetted mode had a lower CO2 stripping performance than the non-wetted mode. Fig. 3c defines the overall wetted mode as the final state of the long-term CO2 stripping operation. Because the pathway of CO2  gas on the membrane surface is filled with liquid that carries the CO2  slowly  rather  than  in  gas,  critical  performance  degradation  can occur [39].

texts.orig: 3.2. Numerical solution of partial differential equations
texts.text: 3.2. Numerical solution of partial differential equations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The theoretical modeling of CO2  stripping was derived from a numerical solution of partial differential equations. The governing partial differential equations are based on the mass balance of CO2 between the liquid and gas phases. A flow diagram of the overall CO2 stripping model is  shown  in  Fig.  5.  The  input  variables  are  determined  by  operating conditions  such  as  membrane  properties,  liquid  flow  rate,  vacuum pressure, and initial pH. The differential CO2 molar flow rate is derived from the diffusion model at each contact stage and represents the interval of the CO2 molar flow rate in the liquid and gas phases in the next stage. After each iteration, the calculated CO2  concentrations at each phase and CO2 stripping flux were identified through the mass balance equation.

The differential CO2 molar flow rate is derived from the diffusion model at each contact stage and represents the interval of the CO2 molar flow rate in the liquid and gas phases in the next stage. After each iteration, the calculated CO2  concentrations at each phase and CO2 stripping flux were identified through the mass balance equation.

The liquid flow rate is known to enhance the advective flux during the CO2 stripping  process  [44];  therefore,  our  experiments  were  performed under various liquid flow rates to determine the relationship between the liquid flow rate and CO2 stripping performance.

The wetting constant is associated with the mass transfer coefficient at the  membrane  phase,  and  it  causes  a  difference  in  the  overall  mass transfer  coefficient  between  the  non-wetted  mode  and  the  overall wetted mode. Fig. 6a and b compare the experimental and modeling results, and no significant change in the wetting constant was observed at  different  liquid  flow  rates.  The  R 2 value  is  associated  with  the modeling accuracy. Under a wetting constant of 0, 0.5, and 1.0, the values were determined to be negative, which indicates that correlation between the experimental data and modeling dramatically decreased. In contrast, the wetting constant of 0.25 derived the highest R 2 value at 0.986. Hence, the wetting constant was determined to be 0.25 (partiallywetted mode) which is within the general range of a PP membrane [45, 46]. Depending on the wetting constant, the overall mass transfer coefficient may vary by approximately 8.7 times (Table 4).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The high liquid flow rate increased the CO2 stripping flux, whereas it decreased the CO2 removal efficiency. The CO2 stripping flux is classified  into  advective  and  diffusive  fluxes  [21].  The  liquid  flow  rate  is associated with the advective flux, and the CO2 stripping flux increases with a higher liquid flow rate (Fig. 6a). The experimental data show that when the liquid flow rate changed from 5 mL/min to 20 mL/min, the CO2 stripping flux increased from 7.31 × 10 5  mol m 2 s 1 to 9.89 × 10 5  mol m 2 s 1 , respectively. However, the CO2  removal efficiency decreased  with  a  higher  liquid  flow  rate  (Fig.  6b).  Because  the liquid-membrane contact time increases with a low liquid flow rate, the amount of stripped CO2 can be greater even with a lower CO2 stripping flux. The CO2 removal efficiencies were 7.32% and 2.47% for liquid flow rates  of  5  mL/min  and  20  mL/min,  respectively.  Therefore,  when determining the liquid flow rate, CO2 stripping flux and the CO2 removal efficiency should be considered.

texts.text: 4.2.1. Effect of vacuum pressure

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Because it is difficult to supply high-purity N2 as sweep gas, the aircombination mode and vacuum mode should be considered for continuous CO2 stripping operations. The CO2 partial pressure in the air was sufficiently low to strip CO2 from the water. In addition, a low vacuum pressure can enhance the difference between the CO2 partial pressure of the  liquid  and  gas  phases.  Thus,  experiments  were  conducted  under various vacuum pressures to identify the effect.
texts.text: Because it is difficult to supply high-purity N2 as sweep gas, the aircombination mode and vacuum mode should be considered for continuous CO2 stripping operations. The CO2 partial pressure in the air was sufficiently low to strip CO2 from the water. In addition, a low vacuum pressure can enhance the difference between the CO2 partial pressure of the  liquid  and  gas  phases.  Thus,  experiments  were  conducted  under various vacuum pressures to identify the effect.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A dramatic change in the CO2 stripping flux was not observed in the air-combination mode at different vacuum pressures. This is because the CO2 partial pressure in the air is sufficiently low to activate CO2  diffusion  without a strong vacuum pressure. When the gas flow rate was maintained as 5000 mL/min, the CO2 stripping flux was changed from 9.51 × 10 5  mol m 2 s 1 to 1.01 × 10 4  mol m 2 s 1 at the vacuum pressure range of 0.3 -0.6 atm (Fig. 7). The CO2  stripping flux determined by theoretical modeling appears to have the same value under each condition, and the experimental data showed corresponding results. Similarly, vacuum conditions did not significantly affect the CO2 stripping flux at gas flow rates of 50 mL/min and 5000 mL/min. The CO2 stripping flux at a gas flow rate of 5 mL/min showed a difference rather than a high gas flow rate in the range of 0.3 -0.6 atm vacuum pressure.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The captured CO2 represents the forms of H2CO3*(aq), HCO3 (aq), and CO3 2 (aq) based  on  the  pH  condition.  The  pH  7  condition  had  only 16.62% H2CO3*(aq) in the dissolved total carbonate. Similarly, pH levels of 6 and 5 have 66.61% and 95.23% H2CO3*(aq)  of the dissolved total carbonate,  respectively  [31,47].  Therefore,  experiments  were  performed  under  various  pH  conditions  to  evaluate  the  effect  on  CO2 stripping.

The  pH  7  condition  had  only 16.62% H2CO3*(aq) in the dissolved total carbonate. Similarly, pH levels of 6 and 5 have 66.61% and 95.23% H2CO3*(aq)  of the dissolved total carbonate,  respectively  [31,47].  Therefore,  experiments  were  performed  under  various  pH  conditions  to  evaluate  the  effect  on  CO2 stripping.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A high concentration of H2CO3*(aq) increased the difference between the CO2 partial pressures in the liquid and gas phases and enhanced the CO2 stripping performance (Fig. 8a). The CO2 stripping flux at pH 6 was 3.93 × 10 4  mol m 2 s 1 , which showed a dramatic increase compared to that at pH 7. The experimental data at different pH under 5 showed CO2 stripping fluxes of 5.29 × 10 4  mol m 2 s 1 , 5.3 × 10 4  mol m 2 s 1 , 5.41 × 10 4  mol m 2 s 1 , and 5.56 × 10 4  mol m 2 s 1 . Because the concentrations of H2CO3*(aq) between pH levels of 2 and 5 were similar, the  conditions  of  pH  under  5  exhibited  similar  CO2  stripping  fluxes. Hence, it is necessary to maintain a pH under 5 to achieve an efficient system. The experimental and theoretical modeling data were slightly different  at  low  pH  because  the  CO2 sensor  was  sensitive  to  bubble generation in the solution.

texts.orig: 4.3. Improvement in membrane process
texts.text: 4.3. Improvement in membrane process

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.3.1. Long-term CO2 stripping performance
texts.text: 4.3.1. Long-term CO2 stripping performance

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It  has  been  reported  that  fouling  and  pore  swelling  cause  performance degradation of the polymer-based membrane in the amine-based process within 7 days [25,48 -50]. Similarly, low pH water can cause inorganic fouling and pore swelling, and the membrane deformation due to CO2  stripping from the water should also be investigated through a long-term experiment. Thus, an experiment was conducted for 168 h without solution looping.
texts.text: It  has  been  reported  that  fouling  and  pore  swelling  cause  performance degradation of the polymer-based membrane in the amine-based process within 7 days [25,48 -50]. Similarly, low pH water can cause inorganic fouling and pore swelling, and the membrane deformation due to CO2  stripping from the water should also be investigated through a long-term experiment. Thus, an experiment was conducted for 168 h without solution looping.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  CO2  stripping  performance  was  maintained  relatively  stable during 5 days in the long-term experiment, after which displayed a slow decrease. Specifically, the stripping flux decreased from 5.48 × 10 4 mol m 2 s 1 initially to 4.47 × 10 5  mol m 2 s 1 over 7 days (Fig. 9a). As compared to previous studies incorporating solvent heating which reported  performance  degradation  within  1 -3  days  [25,48 -50],  pH adjustment was performed to demonstrate higher CO2 stripping stability. However, as variation in the CO2  stripping performance is insufficient to indicate membrane deformation, visualization of the membrane surface using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) was necessary  to  directly  observe  the  anti-fouling  and  anti-pore  swelling phenomena.

However, as variation in the CO2  stripping performance is insufficient to indicate membrane deformation, visualization of the membrane surface using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) was necessary  to  directly  observe  the  anti-fouling  and  anti-pore  swelling phenomena.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane surface deformation can be detected by comparing the membrane surface before and after the operation; however, no fouling or pore swelling occurred. The pores on the membrane surface show the morphology of a typical PP membrane surface, where pores are evenly distributed. Most of the pore diameters on the initial and final membrane surface were measured as 200 -400 nm, and the morphology and arrangement of pores on the final membrane surface were the same as the  initial  membrane  surface  condition.  Hence,  the  performance degradation was derived by the intensified wetting phenomenon during long-term operating time.

texts.text: 4.3.2. Analysis of module configuration

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To develop a CO2 stripping system, it is necessary to determine the arrangement of the membrane modules to create a high-performance design.  Thus,  parallel  and  series  arrangements  were  investigated  by using  theoretical  modeling.  The  membrane  module  for  theoretical modeling has the same properties, such as the characteristics of the fiber used in the experiment.
texts.text: To develop a CO2 stripping system, it is necessary to determine the arrangement of the membrane modules to create a high-performance design.  Thus,  parallel  and  series  arrangements  were  investigated  by using  theoretical  modeling.  The  membrane  module  for  theoretical modeling has the same properties, such as the characteristics of the fiber used in the experiment.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: With  five  modules,  the  parallel  design  can  achieve  a  higher  CO2 stripping performance than a series design (Fig. 10). Comparing the CO2 removal  efficiency  between  the  parallel  and  series  designs  with  five modules each, the CO2 removal efficiencies were 64.22% and 56.73%, respectively.
texts.text: With  five  modules,  the  parallel  design  can  achieve  a  higher  CO2 stripping performance than a series design (Fig. 10). Comparing the CO2 removal  efficiency  between  the  parallel  and  series  designs  with  five modules each, the CO2 removal efficiencies were 64.22% and 56.73%, respectively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The performance difference between parallel and series designs is caused by a change in the influent conditions. In the parallel design, a water stream is separated and flows into five different modules. The pH level was 5 for the influent at each module in the parallel design, and it increases  to  5.49  after  CO2 stripping.  In  contrast,  the  series  design maintains the liquid flow rate and changes the condition of the influent in each module. The pH of the influent in the first module starts at 5, and the pH of the influent in the second module is 5.15. Thus, the strippable CO2  in the series design gradually decreased by passing through the module. The final pH condition after CO2 stripping was 5.39, which is a lower pH than the result of the parallel design.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusion
texts.text: 5. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To improve the CO2 stripping performance, it is necessary to examine the factors affecting the membrane contactors based on experimental and theoretical  analyses.  In  this  study,  the  contributing  factors  were classified and comprehensively analyzed. The results of this study can be summarized as follows:
texts.text: To improve the CO2 stripping performance, it is necessary to examine the factors affecting the membrane contactors based on experimental and theoretical  analyses.  In  this  study,  the  contributing  factors  were classified and comprehensively analyzed. The results of this study can be summarized as follows:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ● Advection: The liquid flow rate is associated with the wetting constant and advective flux. The value of the wetting constant is not observed significant change by the liquid flow rate. The trends in the CO2 stripping flux and CO2 removal efficiency were reversed. A high liquid flow rate produced more stripped CO2 per unit time, indicating better  CO2 stripping  performance.  However,  it  did  not  induce  a dramatic change.
texts.text: Advection: The liquid flow rate is associated with the wetting constant and advective flux. The value of the wetting constant is not observed significant change by the liquid flow rate. The trends in the CO2 stripping flux and CO2 removal efficiency were reversed. A high liquid flow rate produced more stripped CO2 per unit time, indicating better  CO2 stripping  performance.  However,  it  did  not  induce  a dramatic change.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ● Diffusion: Diffusive flux depends on vacuum pressure and pH conditions. The CO2 stripping performance of the air-combination mode did not dramatically change with changes in the vacuum pressure, but  the  effect  of  vacuum  pressure  was  significant  in  the  vacuum mode. Because pH conditions greatly affect CO2  stripping  performance, it is necessary to maintain pH under 5 for highly efficient CO2 stripping performance.

Because pH conditions greatly affect CO2  stripping  performance, it is necessary to maintain pH under 5 for highly efficient CO2 stripping performance.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ● Membrane: Long-term operation and arrangement should be considered to suggest the high-performance operation. Membrane degradation  was  not  observed  in  the  long-term  experiments  with maintaining the CO2 stripping performance. In addition, the parallel design of the membrane module resulted in a high CO2  stripping performance compared to the series design.
